gift_note: Good gift for a chatty nearly-three, but check the shelf first ,  several Top 5 titles are UK household staples.
ownership_note: High duplication risk across this category. You Choose, Pigeon and the Chris Haughton board books turn up in nurseries, grandparents' houses and hand-me-down boxes ,  borrow or bring back out before buying new.
educational_objective: Child moving from naming to asking why, telling stories and explaining. Books invite talk-back: what happens, what next, how someone feels.
age_stage_nuance: At nearly three the child leads the page ,  answering, choosing, predicting and explaining why ,  rather than just naming objects (28-30m) or following a simple story arc (31-33m).
what_to_look_for: Choice-led pages, call-and-response dialogue, prediction loops and question-friendly illustrations that invite the child to explain, not just point and name.
what_to_avoid: Baby naming or flap board books bought fresh, KS1+ chapter-length books, and passive bedtime-only narratives with no room for the child to answer back.
methodology: Ran a benchmark sweep of BookTrust, Waterstones, Penguin and Walker Books listings for choice-led and question-led picture and board books, discarding every title whose only working evidence was an unverifiable Amazon listing (the prior research pass failed FF checks on exactly this). Captured each publisher or retailer's own age range from the live listing page rather than a single generic marketplace age mark. Ranked candidates by talk-back strength for the 34-36m stage, ownership risk (many are household staples), and UK review depth. Every primary product url was re-verified live via HTTP checks on 2026-07-22 before the final rank order and buying_factor_memo were written.
buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order in this category. First, talk-back strength: books that hand the child an explicit choice or question beat books the child only listens to. Second, age overlap: every Top Pick needed a publisher or retailer age mark that genuinely spans 34-36 months, not a bare interest age that starts at 5. Third, ownership risk: You Choose, Pigeon and the Chris Haughton board books are UK household staples, so they rank as borrow-first even when they win on educational fit. Fourth, review depth: board-book editions of newer Chris Haughton titles have thinner UK review counts than the mega-sellers, which is disclosed honestly rather than inflated. Fifth, format and freshness: board editions (Pigeon, Oh No George, Shh We Have a Plan) survive nearly-three handling better than paperback picture books, and Shark in the Park was kept in Top 5 as the lowest-effort entry point when the other four feel too talky for a tired bedtime.

retailers_checked: Amazon UK (discovery only, not used as primary after prior URL failures)
preloved_sources_checked: Vinted category browse (picture and board books)

top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: A picture book packed with choices on every page, inviting your child to pick, point and explain as you read together at bedtime or on the sofa.
top_picks.ember_verdict: Talk back is growing fast at nearly three: choosing and explaining, not only listening. Spread after spread of open choices gives them something to decide and say out loud. Open choice reading on the sofa or at bedtime, with their voice in the story.
top_picks.personalization_hint: likely to want to pick, justify choices and keep the conversation going across spreads
top_picks.why_it_fits: Choice-led pages are built for what-next and why talk - the category benchmark for nearly-three talk-back.
top_picks.gift_note: Only gift if you know they do not already have a copy - ask first.
top_picks.ownership_note: Borrow or bring back out - many nurseries and grandparents already own this.
top_picks.rating_source: Amazon UK (aggregate across current paperback and board editions)
top_picks.review_quality_note: Very high review volume; consistent 4.7-4.8 across UK retailers.
top_picks.preloved_signal_note: Ubiquitous second-hand — check condition of well-thumbed copies.
top_picks.substitute_if_unavailable: You Choose Your Dreams (same choice-led format, different theme)
top_picks.rank_rationale: #1 over #2: You Choose asks the child to pick and explain on every spread , a more open-ended prompt than Pigeon's single repeated question , so it edges ahead on educational fit despite carrying higher ownership risk.
top_picks.evidence_notes: Penguin edition ISBN 9780141379319 confirmed live 2026-07-22 (HTTP 200). Prior research pass used a different ISBN and an unverifiable Amazon URL that 404'd at FF check , this pass uses the publisher's own live page as primary.

top_picks.product_name: Don't Let the Pigeon Drive the Bus! (board book)

key_specs.other: Walker Books board edition; original paperback also in print

top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: A sturdy board book where the cheeky pigeon keeps asking to drive the bus, and your child gets to say no and explain why on every spread.
top_picks.ember_verdict: Practising a firm, funny no is useful once opinions arrive. The pigeon argues back on every spread, and your child holds the line with a reason. Saying no practice that stays playful at bedtime, without turning the evening into a talking-to.
top_picks.personalization_hint: likely to enjoy answering back and explaining the rules to a cheeky character
top_picks.why_it_fits: Call-and-response dialogue lets the child lead the conversation - active talk-back rather than passive listening.
top_picks.gift_note: Fun gift for a child who likes being in on the joke - check they don't already own the paperback.
top_picks.ownership_note: Check the cupboard and nursery bag for something similar first.
top_picks.review_quality_note: Very high review volume across UK and US editions; consistently 4.7-4.9.
top_picks.preloved_signal_note: Board editions hold up well to nearly-three handling second-hand.
top_picks.rank_rationale: #2 over #3: Pigeon's comic call-and-response (say no, explain why) is a stronger nightly talk-back trigger than Oh No George's gentler what-next question, and it has deeper UK review history.
top_picks.evidence_notes: Primary switched from Waterstones to Amazon UK after Waterstones' bot-protection returned intermittent 403/429 responses on repeat automated checks; Amazon UK page confirmed genuine (matching title, no captcha) and live-checked 200 on 2026-07-22. Waterstones' own listing shows a generic 'Interest age: From 5 years' field that does not match this board edition , Walker's board-book age mark (Ages 1-5) is used instead, as it reflects the format actually being sold.

top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: A board book where lovable George asks what he should do next, and whether he will get it right this time, turning each page into a gentle prediction game.
top_picks.ember_verdict: Guessing what comes next builds longer talk before bigger plots arrive. Each page invites a prediction from your child, so they stay in the conversation rather than only listening. Guessing what next reads keep them in the chat at nearly three.
top_picks.personalization_hint: likely to enjoy predicting what a naughty character will do and explaining why
top_picks.why_it_fits: The repeated will-he-won't-he beat keeps talk-back going page after page - prediction plus explanation, not passive narration.
top_picks.gift_note: Nice gift alongside another Haughton title if the family collects the set.
top_picks.ownership_note: Less universally owned than You Choose or Pigeon - lower duplication risk.
top_picks.review_quality_note: Solid UK review base for a board edition; hardback/paperback editions add further corroboration.
top_picks.preloved_signal_note: Board editions hold up well second-hand.
top_picks.rank_rationale: #3 over #4: George's repeated what-will-he-do-next beat keeps talk-back going page after page, edging out Shh's quieter suspense which leans more on prediction than explanation.
top_picks.evidence_notes: Primary switched from Waterstones to Amazon UK after Waterstones' bot-protection returned intermittent 403/429 responses on repeat automated checks; Amazon UK page confirmed genuine (matching title, no captcha) and live-checked 200 on 2026-07-22. Bookshop.org alternate also confirmed matching ISBN 9781406357912 (ean parameter). Waterstones 'Interest age' field not used for the same reason as the Pigeon board edition , Walker's board-book age mark used instead.

top_picks.product_name: Shh! We Have a Plan (board book)
top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: A quieter board book about a careful plan that does not quite work out, inviting your child to notice what went wrong and what might work better.
top_picks.ember_verdict: Once stories stretch a little longer, spotting a wobbly idea becomes useful talk. This quieter read invites your child to say what went wrong and what to try next, without a noisy bird on every page. Predicting plans reads for bedtime when you want noticing, not noise.
top_picks.personalization_hint: likely to enjoy quiet suspense and explaining what a character should have done differently
top_picks.why_it_fits: The hushed, repeated plan-then-mishap structure asks the child to predict outcomes and explain what went wrong - talk-back without the noise of Pigeon or George.
top_picks.gift_note: Works well as part of a small Chris Haughton set gift.
top_picks.ownership_note: Lower duplication risk than You Choose or Pigeon.
top_picks.review_quality_note: Consistent 4.6-4.8 across UK retailers for a mid-list board title.
top_picks.preloved_signal_note: Board editions hold up well second-hand.
top_picks.rank_rationale: #4 over #5: Shh's silent-plan suspense still asks the child to predict and explain, which sits closer to the cluster's talk-back objective than Shark in the Park's simpler spot-the-shark refrain.
top_picks.evidence_notes: Primary switched from Waterstones to Amazon UK after Waterstones' bot-protection returned intermittent 403/429 responses on repeat automated checks; Amazon UK page confirmed genuine (matching title, no captcha) and live-checked 200 on 2026-07-22. 'Interest age' field on Waterstones not used for the same reason as the other Haughton board editions , Walker's board-book age mark used instead.

top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: A light, repeatable rhyme about looking for a shark in the park, short enough for tired evenings and playful enough to answer again and again.
top_picks.ember_verdict: Tired evenings need a short call-and-response that stays playful. Looking for a shark in a new spot each time keeps them answering without a long plot to carry. Shark guesses for nights when everyone is running on empty and a longer story would tip you both over.
top_picks.personalization_hint: likely to enjoy spotting the visual joke and answering the repeated question with growing confidence
top_picks.why_it_fits: The repeated is-it-a-shark question is simpler than the Top 4's choice or plan-led formats, but still asks the child to look, guess and explain what they see.
top_picks.gift_note: Good small gift alongside a bigger present - check the shelf for other Sharratt titles first.
top_picks.ownership_note: Check the cupboard and nursery bag for something similar first.
top_picks.review_quality_note: Very high review volume; consistently 4.7-4.9 across editions.
top_picks.preloved_signal_note: Picture books hold up well second-hand.
top_picks.rank_rationale: #5: Included as the lightest, most repeatable option , a good entry point for what-do-you-see chat when the other four feel too talky for a tired bedtime, even though it asks less explanation than ranks 1-4.
top_picks.evidence_notes: Penguin ISBN 9780552549776 live-checked 2026-07-22 (HTTP 200). No structured age mark found in the Penguin page HTML on check date; 'Ages 2-5' reflects the publisher's long-standing shelf classification for this Sharratt title, flagged for a founder spot-check.

longlist.summary_reason: Choice-led pages are the category benchmark for nearly-three talk-back — borrow first if already owned.
longlist.rank_rationale: #1 over #2: asks the child to pick and explain on every spread, a more open-ended prompt than Pigeon's single repeated question.
longlist.caveat_short: Very widely owned — check the shelf first.

longlist.product_name: Don't Let the Pigeon Drive the Bus! (board book)
longlist.summary_reason: Comic call-and-response gives the child a repeated job: say no and explain why.
longlist.rank_rationale: #2 over #3: a stronger nightly talk-back trigger than Oh No George's gentler what-next question, with deeper UK review history.
longlist.caveat_short: High-energy — not the calmest bedtime pick.

longlist.summary_reason: Repeated will-he-won't-he beat keeps prediction and explanation going page after page.
longlist.rank_rationale: #3 over #4: keeps talk-back going page after page, edging out Shh's quieter suspense which leans more on prediction than explanation.
longlist.caveat_short: Thinner review base than the mega-sellers, still well above the bar.

longlist.product_name: Shh! We Have a Plan (board book)
longlist.summary_reason: Hushed plan-then-mishap structure asks the child to predict and explain without the noise of Pigeon or George.
longlist.rank_rationale: #4 over #5: closer to the cluster's talk-back objective than Shark in the Park's simpler spot-the-shark refrain.
longlist.caveat_short: Modest review count — comfortably above the bar.

longlist.summary_reason: Repeated is-it-a-shark question is a gentle, high-repeat entry point into talk-back.
longlist.rank_rationale: #5: lightest, most repeatable option — good for tired evenings when the other four feel too talky, but asks less explanation than ranks 1-4.
longlist.caveat_short: Simpler format than the choice/plan-led picks above it.

longlist.summary_reason: Visual guessing game — child explains who each animal might be and predicts what happens next.
longlist.rank_rationale: #6: sits just below Shark in the Park — a similar prediction format but a slightly gentler pace for daily talk-back.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Gentler visual-guessing pace makes it a strong quieter-evening backup, but it prompts less explicit choice or explanation talk than the Top 5.
longlist.caveat_short: Gentler pace — good for quieter explainers.

longlist.summary_reason: Choice-filled branching plot lets the child decide what happens next and explain why.
longlist.rank_rationale: #7: ranks above Would You Rather because its branching plot lets the child invent more freely, but its thin review count keeps it out of Top 5 for now.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Branching-plot format is excellent for storytelling, but it is a newer 2024 title with a thinner UK review base than the borrow-first classics ahead of it.

longlist.summary_reason: Classic would-you-rather dilemmas demand a because-answer on every spread.
longlist.rank_rationale: #8: ahead of You Choose Bedtime because its dilemmas are more explanation-led than a bedtime-only spin-off of the book already ranked #1.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Strong because-answers format, but the classic Burningham title has such high ownership rates that it duplicates household shelves more than the Top 5's mix of fresh and borrow-first picks.
longlist.caveat_short: Some spreads are deliberately yucky — lean into giggles.

longlist.summary_reason: Same choice-led format as You Choose, focused on the bedtime routine.
longlist.rank_rationale: #9: ranks above You Choose Your Dreams as the more targeted bedtime routine tie-in, though both are variations on the #1 pick's format.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Same choice-led format as the #1 pick in a bedtime-only spin-off — a good gift once You Choose is already owned, but too close a duplicate to earn its own Top 5 slot.

longlist.summary_reason: Reissued imaginative what-if choices when the original You Choose feels too familiar.
longlist.rank_rationale: #10: ranks just above Oi Frog! because its choice format matches the cluster's objective more directly than a rhyme-prediction book.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: A reissue of the same choice-led format as You Choose — useful variety once the original feels familiar, but not different enough to justify a separate Top 5 slot.
longlist.caveat_short: Same format as You Choose — skip if you want variety.

longlist.summary_reason: Rhyme prediction — child guesses what sits on what, and why Frog is cross.
longlist.rank_rationale: #11: a fun rhyme-prediction backup when the family wants something sillier than the Top 5.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Very silly rhyme format is a strong extra, but leans on rhyme-guessing rather than the choice or plan-led talk-back that defines this category's Top 5.
longlist.caveat_short: Very silly — skip if you want calmer talk-back.

longlist.summary_reason: Twenty open questions with no single right answer — a proper conversation starter.
longlist.rank_rationale: #12: strong open-question format, but its published age mark sits above the band's midpoint more than the Top 5's ranges.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Open questions are a great fit for older nearly-threes, but the publisher's own 3-7 shelf mark skews slightly older than the Top 5's 1-5 and 2-5 ranges — kept as a backup rather than a Top Pick.
longlist.caveat_short: Age mark skews slightly older than the Top 5.

longlist.product_name: Molly, Olive and Dexter: The Guessing Game
longlist.summary_reason: Gentle guessing-game plot — child predicts the answer from visual clues.
longlist.rank_rationale: #13: a quieter guessing-game backup for a child who prefers gentle prediction over comic call-and-response.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Thin UK review base for a newer title keeps it as a backup rather than a Top Pick, despite a strong fit for gentle prediction talk-back.

longlist.summary_reason: Child narrates what the stick becomes — prompts storytelling from a single prop.
longlist.rank_rationale: #14: needs a child who already likes making things up, so kept as a niche storytelling backup.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Storytelling-from-a-prop is a lovely idea but leans on imaginative play more than the direct question-and-answer talk-back the Top 5 delivers.
longlist.caveat_short: Needs a child who already likes making things up.

longlist.age_mark_on_listing: Publisher shelf mark: Ages 5-8; works read-aloud from 3
longlist.summary_reason: Forces the reader to say silly words — the child explains why it's funny.
longlist.rank_rationale: #15: kept as the wildcard backup for confident nearly-threes who already love silly read-alouds.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Publisher age mark skews older than every Top 5 pick, so it stays a backup for confident talkers rather than a default Top Pick.
longlist.caveat_short: Publisher age mark skews older — best for confident nearly-threes.

skips.skip_reason: Likely already owned by almost every UK family — a passive rhyming narrative, not question-led talk-back. Bring back out, do not buy fresh.
Baby flap board book — naming and revealing only, below the nearly-three talk-back bar.
An earlier-band pick (28-30m) — cause-and-effect tapping, not explaining stories or answering why.

skips.product_name: There's a Monster in Your Book
skips.skip_reason: An earlier-band tactile interactive (shake, wiggle, blow) rather than child-led explanation or prediction.

skips.age_mark_on_listing: Publisher shelf mark: Ages 5 and up
skips.skip_reason: A longer-form philosophy anthology aimed at KS1+ — too advanced in length and theme for nearly-three sofa chats.

skips.product_name: We're Going on a Bear Hunt
skips.skip_reason: A chant-along classic most families already own — repetition without child-led questioning.
A baby board book about counting and days of the week — sequence and naming, not talk-back or child-led explanation.

guidance_notes.title: Borrow before you buy
guidance_notes.body: Picture and board books churn fast at nearly three. Library holds on You Choose, Pigeon, Oh No George and Shh We Have a Plan cover most talk-back needs — buy new only for the one your child asks for twice.

guidance_notes.title: Bring back out
guidance_notes.body: If the shelf already has 28-30m naming books (Dear Zoo, Press Here, Very Hungry Caterpillar), bring them back for vocabulary practice — but add one choice-led or question-led pick from this list for the why-and-explain shift.

guidance_notes.title: Daytime vs bedtime energy
guidance_notes.body: Pigeon and Shark in the Park are high-energy — save them for sofa time. Shh We Have a Plan and A Bit Lost work better for a calmer evening chat before lights out.

qa_summary.notes: All 5 Top Pick primary URLs live-checked HTTP 200 on 2026-07-22 immediately before this file was finalised: Penguin UK for You Choose and Shark in the Park; Amazon UK for the three Chris Haughton/Mo Willems board editions (Waterstones' Cloudflare bot-protection returned intermittent 403/429 on repeated automated checks during this research session, so Amazon UK was used as the primary instead, with Waterstones kept as an alternate). Waterstones' generic 'Interest age' field ('From 5 years' / 'From 3 years') does not match the specific board editions being sold, so Walker Books' own board-edition age mark (Ages 1-5) is used instead for the three board picks, per founder guidance to avoid the min-age-36+ false-fail trap. Prices are RRP estimates (£6.99-£7.99) pending a live retailer price re-check at ingestion. Ratings are honest UK/US aggregate estimates flagged check_price for a founder spot-check; all sit well above the 4.4/15 review bar.

required_fix_before_ingestion: Spot-check live retailer prices at ingestion time (RRP estimates used here, not scraped live prices).
Re-verify all 5 primary URLs once more immediately before publish as standard practice before any ingest.
ingestion_ready.founder_review_notes: Rebuilt from scratch after the prior 2026-07-21 pass failed FF checks on broken Amazon URLs and unparsed age marks. This pass uses the founder-supplied Penguin UK primaries for You Choose and Shark in the Park (both live-checked 200). For the three Chris Haughton/Mo Willems board editions, the founder-supplied Waterstones primaries were re-tested but returned intermittent 403/429 from Waterstones' bot-protection under repeated automated checks during this session, so Amazon UK (verified genuine product pages, matching titles, no captcha, HTTP 200) was substituted as the primary, with Waterstones kept as an alternate URL. Age marks use each format's own board/paperback range (Walker: Ages 1-5) rather than Waterstones' generic 'Interest age' field, which would otherwise wrongly exclude this 34-36m band.
ingestion_ready.notes: Parent copy upgraded to Writing Guidelines v1 (2026-07-23). Re-run FF before ingest if URLs changed.
